title: Chapter Nine: The Lord of Desolate Sect
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

"Everyone gather!"

The examiner's voice was iron.

"Now you need only pass one more gate to become martial artists of the Desolate Sect. I tell you in advance — you will learn and train, carry out missions, and live together in this fortress standing alone in the martial world. Maintain proper dignity and become martial artists befitting your status."

Twenty examinees stood in silence.

The final gate was an interview with the Lord of Desolate Sect.

The living history of the Great Ming Empire. The world's greatest martial arts. A peerless master who had, it was said, kept her youthful appearance for two hundred years. Even for Yeon-shin, meeting such a figure was not a light thing.

They were led through the fortress without a word. No one spoke as they passed magnificent palace halls under the examiner's guidance. The columns were carved with a precision no human hand could have achieved. The energy within pressed against Yeon-shin's skin, a constant low hum resonating through his bones.

The main palace sat at the very centre of the vast compound. It was the largest structure he had ever seen. An overwhelming size.

One by one, examinees were called up. Each came down dazed, blank-faced, as if they had glimpsed something beyond comprehension. The intervals were heartbeats, not minutes — so short it seemed the Lord merely looked at their faces and sent them away.

*What kind of being can assess a warrior's soul that quickly?*

The burning at his crown intensified. He pressed his thumb to the top of his head. A habitual gesture now.

"Jeong Yeon-shin."

He climbed the spiralling stairs. A hundred steps. More. They seemed to go on endlessly. The stone was cool beneath his hand. The energy in the stairwell grew heavier with every step, pressing down like a physical weight.

*They said to go all the way up.*

At the top, an antique and massive door. It opened on its own as he approached.

The office was trees.

Not decorated with them. Made of them. Huge living trunks twisted naturally and rose to form what appeared to be desks, tea tables, beds. Branches and foliage everywhere, green and breathing. The walls were wide open to the sky, and pure sunlight poured through.

Truly bizarre.

"I am Jeong Yeon-shin."

His voice did not waver.

A figure sat among the trees. Her martial robes seemed dyed the pale green of leaves steeped in water. She leaned against a trunk, partially obscured by leafy branches.

She raised her head.

Their eyes met.

Something rose through him — beyond a shudder, beyond awe. A sensation that pressed against the inside of his skull and made the burning at his crown flare and subside in a single pulse. Her eyes were deep green, and meeting them was like staring into a well that had no bottom. Eyes that seemed to encompass the principles of all things.

*The world's greatest. The Lord of Desolate Sect.*

Her pointed ears, like the tip of a divine sword. Her beauty, rarely seen in the mortal realm. Those were not the essence.

The eyes were.

He had a strong feeling that even the Tyrant Sword Lord, the absolute being in his memories, could not escape those eyes. A being of a different dimension entirely. It was no wonder the interviews were so short — she saw everything in a glance.

A gust of wind lifted her long green hair and brushed Yeon-shin's cheek.

"You... are that child's bloodline," she said.

"By bloodline, you mean —"

"It seems you already know. You have a naive side."

Her voice was calm. Ancient.

"I am the grandson of the previous Divine Sword Squad Leader."

"He is a stubborn child, even though he will soon take the seat of the Elder Council. You carry a wound."

"It is not a matter the Sect Leader should concern herself with. Just a trivial family affair."

"Such things are not trivial." Her green gaze held him, reaching through skin and bone to read what lay beneath. "It is hidden by your well-trained body, but you seem to have lived only about fifteen years. However — an outsider cannot intervene. Family matters are for family."

"I apologise."

He said it with a face showing neither regret nor discomfort. He thought that the transcendent aura of the absolute being and the matter of his family were separate things.

As if sensing his thoughts, the corners of her mouth rose.

"I like it. The spirit to not yield, even though you came here with a desire."

*She saw through it. Of course she did.*

The abnormally open Baihui acupoint. His condition, his shortened lifespan. A supreme master like the Sect Leader would read it at a glance.

"Can someone like me eat the fruit of the World Tree and live?"

The words came out steady. Plain.

"The efficacy may be somewhat distorted. But yes. You will be able to achieve what you desire."

"Can it be obtained?"

Her smile deepened.

"That is up to you."

"That is enough for now. Thank you."

He bowed with hands clasped. Then asked one more thing.

"Am I now truly a warrior of the Desolate Sect?"

"Yes. You may go down now." A pause. Something like amusement in her ancient eyes. "Future Divine Sword Squad Leader."

He could not tell if she was teasing or truly acknowledging what she saw.

*It also sounds like she is saying I can obtain the fruit if I become the Divine Sword Squad Leader.*

He left the office of trees. Behind him, a gust of wind stirred the leaves, and the door closed on its own.

---

At the bottom, Won-chang was vibrating with anxiety.

"What happened? You took so long! What did the Sect Leader say? Did you pass?"

"Personal matters. Of course I passed. But where did everyone go?"

"Everyone left in a daze except Master Namgung! I'm so anxious I could die!"

Across the hall, Namgung Hwa-shin met his eye and smiled. Yeon-shin returned the greeting. When he turned back, Won-chang was already climbing the stairs, grey-faced, with the demeanour of a man walking to the gallows.

"It's nothing much," Yeon-shin called after him.

A weak wave.

Won-chang came back grinning.

"Hahaha! Desolate Sect! I am a Desolate Sect warrior!"

His whispered cheering — mindful of the hallowed halls — was entirely untrustworthy. But Yeon-shin honestly had no idea about the passing criteria. The Desolate Sect seemed to have its doors open wider than he had expected.

A scholar appeared. Middle-aged, composed, with a scholarly air that belied the practised authority in his voice.

"Welcome to the sect. I am Gi Dae-seung, under Headquarters. I will guide you to your lodgings while affiliations are assigned."

"When will they be decided?"

"The Squad Leaders will select you. At least seven days and nights."

Four had passed. Yeon-shin, Namgung Hwa-shin, Won-chang, and one other swordsman. They followed the scholar through the grounds.

"Do you know what the Desolate Sect mainly does?" Gi Dae-seung asked.

"Preventing rebellions by supreme masters?" Won-chang offered.

"Correct in part, but rather than mainly doing that, it is something that must be done on a large scale when needed. More frequent is preventing or suppressing murim conflicts above a certain scale. The Namgung Clan's events have been blocked numerous times."

"Yes," Namgung Hwa-shin said. Brief. No bitterness.

Yeon-shin glanced at him. His expression was better than expected. He had left a prestigious family with circumstances outsiders would find hard to understand, and he walked forward without a trace of resentment.

*We have this in common.*

The scholar continued, his tone shifting. "Fights in the murim are unpredictable. When rumours spread about a heavenly demon's tomb, a bloody storm sweeps through the Central Plains. The tyranny of vicious martial artists does not distinguish between enemies and common folk. The chaos is unimaginable — a world apart from the order of the Great Ming Empire."

"The Chaos of the Heavenly Demon's Tomb," Won-chang murmured. His face was solemn — one of those rare moments of seriousness.

"Seventeen years ago. The suffering was beyond imagining." The scholar sighed. "That is why the imperial court provides massive wealth, divine medicines, secret manuals, and masters. What you must do in return is behead those who disrupt the people's lives."

"We lack people," he added. "Talents with great potential who can be trusted are rare. There is much work, and the scope is vast. That is why it takes time to allocate the selected to the right places."

They reached the palace where they would stay. Each person had their own high-class room. A training ground behind the building. Imperial-level luxury.

*Jeong Estate was a stable compared to this.*

Seven days and nights passed. Yeon-shin trained every waking hour. The palace was even more luxurious than it appeared from outside — proof of the massive support the imperial court provided.

Affiliations were assigned. The four parted ways.

"I pray for your martial fortune," Namgung Hwa-shin said.

"And you, Master Namgung."

Farewell.

Strange rumours had circulated during the wait. The chiefs of the seventeen martial divisions under the Divine Sword Corps had sparred among themselves — not against enemies, but each other — to determine who would recruit the new warriors. Two names on every tongue.

White Qilin and Lightning Flash.

---
